Details: Similar to other advanced oxidation technologies that create hydroxyl radicals, ultraviolet irradiation combined with ozone was found to be of varying effectiveness at removing 1,4-dioxane (7.6 to greater than 98 percent removal). Data suggest that the removal efficiency depends on UV dose, ozone dose, contact time, and initial 1,4-dioxane concentration. Most of the studies treated water with high 1,4-dioxane concentrations (55 to 500 milligrams per liter) and employed ozone doses and/or UV reaction times greater than typical of drinking water treatment [2475, 2476, 2477]. One bench scale study, however, achieved 78 percent removal of a low initial 1,4-dioxane concentration (20 micrograms per liter) using more typical ozone dose and UV reaction time [2479].
